sources.reviewedCode:
# Time:  O(n)
# Space: O(n)

# tree
# [Reader note] Entry point
# Start from the core routine and keep this mental model in view: Construct nodes and edges first, then ask which node was never claimed as anyone else's child.
class Solution(object):
    def createBinaryTree(self, descriptions):
        """
        :type descriptions: List[List[int]]
        :rtype: Optional[TreeNode]
        """
        # [Reader note] State setup
        # This setup stage creates the state needed for the invariant or partial result: Node creation, parent-child wiring, and root detection from incomplete relational data.
        nodes = {}
        children = set()
        # [Reader note] Main pass
        # This is the main sweep where the implementation earns its efficiency instead of restarting work from scratch.
        for p, c, l in descriptions:
            parent = nodes.setdefault(p, TreeNode(p))
            child = nodes.setdefault(c, TreeNode(c))
            # [Reader note] Decision point
            # Pay attention here: The node wiring is not difficult, but it is easy to lose track of which values have already been established as children.
            if l:
                parent.left = child
            else:
                parent.right = child
            children.add(c)
        # [Reader note] Return
        # The final return exposes the result after the traversal and state updates have settled.
        return nodes[next(p for p in nodes.iterkeys() if p not in children)]

sources.reviewedCode:
# Definition for a binary tree node.
# class TreeNode:
# [Reader note] Entry point
# Start from the core routine and keep this mental model in view: Construct nodes and edges first, then ask which node was never claimed as anyone else's child.
#     def __init__(self, val=0, left=None, right=None):
# [Reader note] State setup
# This setup stage creates the state needed for the invariant or partial result: Node creation, parent-child wiring, and root detection from incomplete relational data.
#         self.val = val
#         self.left = left
#         self.right = right
class Solution:
    def createBinaryTree(self, descriptions: List[List[int]]) -> Optional[TreeNode]:
        nodes = defaultdict(TreeNode)
        children = set()
        # [Reader note] Main pass
        # This is the main sweep where the implementation earns its efficiency instead of restarting work from scratch.
        for parent, child, isLeft in descriptions:
            # [Reader note] Decision point
            # Pay attention here: The node wiring is not difficult, but it is easy to lose track of which values have already been established as children.
            if parent not in nodes:
                nodes[parent] = TreeNode(parent)
            if child not in nodes:
                nodes[child] = TreeNode(child)
            children.add(child)
            if isLeft:
                nodes[parent].left = nodes[child]
            else:
                nodes[parent].right = nodes[child]
        root = (set(nodes.keys()) - children).pop()
        # [Reader note] Return
        # The final return exposes the result after the traversal and state updates have settled.
        return nodes[root]
